revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 熊野若王子神社 |所在地= [[京都府]][[京都市]][[左京区]]若王子町2 | 緯度度 = | 経度度 = | 地図国コード = |祭神 = [[国之常立神|国常立神]]、[[イザナギ|伊佐那岐神]]、[[イザナミ|伊佐那美神]]、[[天照大神|天照皇大神]] |創建 = [[永暦]]元年（[[1160年]]） |社格 = |札所等 = |本殿 = |例祭 = 7月10日 |神事 = |画像 = |地図 = }} '''熊野若王子神社'''（くまのにゃくおうじじんじゃ）は、[[京都市]][[左京区]]若王子町にある[[神社]]。かつては[[禅林寺]]の鎮守社であった。[[哲学の道]]の南端にある。京都三熊野の一つ。
== 歴史 == [[永暦]]元年（[[1160年]]）に[[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]が[[禅林寺]]の鎮守として[[熊野権現]]を勧請したことに始まる。社名は[[天照大神|天照皇大神]]の別名・若一王子から採られたものである。
[[熊野詣]]を行うにあたり、[[修験者]]はまず当社で身を浄めてから出発したという。
[[足利尊氏]]、[[足利義政]]がこの地にて宴を開いたとされている。
社殿が衰微していたため、[[明治]]時代に修理が行われている。当社の本殿は[[熊野本宮大社|本宮]]、[[熊野速玉大社|新宮]]、[[熊野那智大社|那智]]、[[若宮]]の4社で構成されていたが、[[1978年]]（[[昭和]]53年）に一社相殿の本殿にまとめられた。
京都府で最も古い梛（[[ナギ]]）の大木（樹齢400余年）がある。
京都には京都三熊野といわれる神社があり、それぞれ[[新熊野神社]]は[[熊野本宮大社]]、[[熊野神社 (京都市)|熊野神社]]は[[熊野速玉大社]]、熊野若王子神社は[[熊野那智大社]]というように[[熊野三山]]に対応している。
== 祭神 == * 主祭神 - [[国之常立神|国常立神]]、[[イザナギ|伊佐那岐神]]、[[イザナミ|伊佐那美神]]、[[天照大神|天照皇大神]]
== 境内 == * 本殿 - [[1978年]]（[[昭和]]53）再建。 * 拝殿 * 恵比須社 * 社務所
== 祭事 == * 例祭・7月10日
== 参考文献 == * 熊野若王子神社由緒
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.kyoto-16sha.jp/kumanonyakuojijinja//京都十六社朱印めぐり 京都 熊野若王子神社]
{{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くまのにゃくおうししんしや}} [[Category:京都市の神社]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:禅林寺]] [[Category:左京区の建築物]] [[Category:左京区の歴史]]

{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社}}[[ja:熊野若王子神社]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji ]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Images]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[Category:New-]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Shortest 250 pages as of 2025-02-19]] [[zh:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] {{Shrine |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |Location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |Image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |Deity = {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Zenrin-ji (Kyoto)|ja|禅林寺}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Path|ja|哲学の道}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} at {{ill|Zenrin-ji (Kyoto)|ja|禅林寺}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10

[[ja:熊野若王子神社]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji ]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Shrine stubs]] [[Category:Images]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[Category:New-]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Shortest 250 pages as of 2025-02-19]] [[zh:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] {{Shrine |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |Location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |Image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |Deity = {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} at {{ill|Zenrin-ji (Kyoto)|ja|禅林寺}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10

In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.

[[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:null category]][[Category:new pages most likely untranslated or similar]] [[Category:Shortest 250 pages as of 2025-02-19]] [[zh:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10

[[Category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:new pages most likely untranslated or similar]] [[Category:Shortest 250 pages as of 2025-02-19]] [[zh:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10

[[Category:qqqqq]] [[Category:new pages most likely untranslated or similar]] [[Category:Shortest 250 pages as of 2025-02-19]] [[zh:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10

[[Category:qq]] [[Category:new pages most likely untranslated or similar]] [[Category:Shortest 250 pages as of 2025-02-19]] [[zh:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Sources ==
* Shrine history from Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine.
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}
== External Links ==
{{Commonscat|Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja}} * [http://www.kyoto-16sha.jp/kumanonyakuojijinja/ Kyoto 16 Shrine Pilgrimage – Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine] (in Japanese)

'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10

'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10

'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|後白河天皇}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|室町幕府}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10

[[Category:11]] [[Category:Shortest 250 pages]][[Category:as of 2025-02-19]] [[zh:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10
== References ==
{{Reflist}}

* Shrine history from Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine.

[[Category:11]] [[Category:Shortest 250 pages]][[Category:as of 2025-02-19]] [[zh:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kunitokotachi no Kami|ja|国之常立神}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10

[[Category:11]] [[Category:Shortest 250 pages]][[Category:as of 2025-02-19]] [[zh:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] [[en:Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区|12=draft}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|12=draft}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦|12=draft}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|12=draft}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社|12=draft}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区|12=draft}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|12=draft}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺|12=draft}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道|12=draft}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野|12=draft}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦|12=draft}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|12=draft}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=draft}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺|12=draft}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=draft}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|12=draft}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者|12=draft}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|12=draft}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正|12=draft}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政|12=draft}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|12=draft}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|12=draft}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|12=draft}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|12=draft}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮|12=draft}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ|12=draft}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|12=draft}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社|12=draft}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|12=draft}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|12=draft}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|12=draft}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|12=draft}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Sources ==
* Shrine history from Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine.
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=draft}}
== External Links ==

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区|12=draft}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦|12=draft}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|12=draft}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社|12=draft}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区|12=draft}}, Kyoto City. Its former rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|12=draft}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺|12=draft}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道|12=draft}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野|12=draft}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦|12=draft}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|12=draft}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=draft}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺|12=draft}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=draft}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者|12=draft}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|12=draft}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正|12=draft}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政|12=draft}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|12=draft}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|12=draft}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|12=draft}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|12=draft}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮|12=draft}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ|12=draft}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|12=draft}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社|12=draft}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|12=draft}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|12=draft}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|12=draft}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Sources ==
* Shrine history from Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine.
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=draft}}
== External Links ==
{{Commonscat|Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja}} * [http://www.kyoto-16sha.jp/kumanonyakuojijinja/ Kyoto 16 Shrine Pilgrimage – Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine] (in Japanese)

revision.comment: 15 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:熊野若王子神社 up to rev 93833456
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 熊野若王子神社 |所在地= [[京都府]][[京都市]][[左京区]]若王子町2 |画像 = [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|拝殿]] | 緯度度 = | 経度度 = | 地図国コード = |祭神 = [[国之常立神|国常立神]]、[[イザナギ|伊佐那岐神]]、[[イザナミ|伊佐那美神]]、[[天照大神|天照皇大神]] |創建 = [[永暦]]元年（[[1160年]]） |社格 = 旧[[村社]] |札所等 = |本殿 = |例祭 = 7月10日 |神事 = |地図 = }} '''熊野若王子神社'''（くまのにゃくおうじじんじゃ）は、[[京都市]][[左京区]]若王子町にある[[神社]]。旧社格は[[村社]]。かつては[[禅林寺 (京都市)|禅林寺]]の鎮守社であった。[[哲学の道]]の南端にある。通称、若王子神社。京都三熊野の一つ。
== 歴史 == [[永暦]]元年（[[1160年]]）に[[後白河天皇|後白河法皇]]が[[禅林寺 (京都市)|禅林寺]]の鎮守として[[熊野権現]]を勧請したことに始まる。社名は[[天照大神|天照皇大神]]の別名・[[若一王子]]から採られたものである。
[[熊野詣]]を行うにあたり、[[修験者]]はまず当社で身を浄めてから出発したという。
[[足利尊氏]]、[[寛正]]6年（[[1465年]]）3月には[[足利義政]]がこの地で花見をし、宴を開いている。また、[[室町幕府]]や武家からの信仰を集めた。
[[応仁の乱]]で荒廃したが、[[豊臣秀吉]]により再興されている。
[[明治]]時代には社殿が衰微していたため、修理が行われている。また、[[村社]]に列せられている。当社の本殿は[[熊野本宮大社|本宮]]、[[熊野速玉大社|新宮]]、[[熊野那智大社|那智]]、[[若宮]]の4社で構成されていたが、[[1979年]]（[[昭和]]54年）に一社相殿の本殿にまとめられた。
京都府で最も古い梛（[[ナギ]]）の大木（樹齢400余年）がある。
京都には京都三熊野といわれる神社があり、それぞれ[[新熊野神社]]は[[熊野本宮大社]]、[[熊野神社 (京都市)|熊野神社]]は[[熊野速玉大社]]、熊野若王子神社は[[熊野那智大社]]というように[[熊野三山]]に対応している。
== 祭神 == * 主祭神 - [[国之常立神|国常立神]]、[[イザナギ|伊佐那岐神]]、[[イザナミ|伊佐那美神]]、[[天照大神|天照皇大神]]
== 境内 == * 本殿 - [[1979年]]（[[昭和]]54年）再建。 * 拝殿 * 恵比須社 * 社務所
== 祭事 == * 例祭 - 7月10日
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 熊野若王子神社由緒
== 関連項目 == * [[若一王子]]
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja}} * [http://www.kyoto-16sha.jp/kumanonyakuojijinja/ 京都十六社朱印めぐり 京都 熊野若王子神社]
{{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くまのにゃくおうししんしや}} [[Category:京都市の神社]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:鎮守社]] [[Category:左京区の建築物]] [[Category:左京区の歴史]] [[Category:京都府の旧村社]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区}}, Kyoto City. Its former Rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Sources ==
* Shrine history from Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine.
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子}}
== External Links ==

* {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}}

* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区|qq=draft}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦|qq=draft}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社|qq=draft}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区|qq=draft}}, Kyoto City. Its former Rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺|qq=draft}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道|qq=draft}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野|qq=draft}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦|qq=draft}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺|qq=draft}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|qq=draft}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者|qq=draft}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|qq=draft}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正|qq=draft}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政|qq=draft}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|qq=draft}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|qq=draft}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮|qq=draft}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ|qq=draft}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|qq=draft}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社|qq=draft}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|qq=draft}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|qq=draft}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|qq=draft}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10

* {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto City|qq=}}, {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyō Ward|qq=}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuni-no-Tokotachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|qq=}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiryaku|qq=}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|qq=}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|qq=}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Sakyō Ward|ja|左京区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakyō Ward|qq=}}, Kyoto City. Its former Rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|qq=}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|qq=}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|ja|哲学の道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philosopher's Walk|qq=}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|qq=}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiryaku|qq=}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|qq=}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Ōji|qq=}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pilgrimages to Kumano|qq=}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugenja|qq=}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikaga Takauji|qq=}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanshō|qq=}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikaga Yoshimasa|qq=}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyotomi Hideyoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|qq=}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hongū Taisha|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya|qq=}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagi tree|qq=}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-Kumano Shrine|qq=}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hongū Taisha|qq=}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qq=}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuni-no-Tokotachi|qq=}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|qq=}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Sources ==
* Shrine history from Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine.
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Ōji|qq=}}
== External Links ==

== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|ja|永暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiryaku|qq=}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} at {{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|ja|禅林寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|qq=}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Ōji|qq=}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pilgrimages to Kumano|qq=}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugenja|qq=}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|ja|足利尊氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikaga Takauji|qq=}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanshō|qq=}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|ja|足利義政|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikaga Yoshimasa|qq=}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyotomi Hideyoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|qq=}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hongū Taisha|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya|qq=}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nagi tree|ja|ナギ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagi tree|qq=}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-Kumano Shrine|qq=}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hongū Taisha|qq=}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qq=}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nachi Taisha|qq=}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuni-no-Tokotachi|qq=}} * {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|qq=}} * {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office

Its former Rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|qq=}}. It was once the guardian shrine of [[:en:Zenrin-ji|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji]] and is situated at the southern end of [[:en:Philosopher's Walk|Philosopher's Walk]]. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|qq=}}.
== History ==
In [[:en:Eiryaku|Eiryaku]] 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} at [[:en:Zenrin-ji|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji]], establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Ōji|qq=}}, an alternative name for [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]].

[[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and later, in [[:en:Kanshō|Kanshō]] 6 (1465), [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimasa|Ashikaga Yoshimasa]], held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], it was later restored by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]].
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|qq=}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—[[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]], [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], and [[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]]—but in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred [[:en:Nageia nagi|Nagi tree]], estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-Kumano Shrine|qq=}}, associated with [[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]]; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}}, associated with [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]].

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō Ward]], 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]], [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]], [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] |Established = [[:en:Eiryaku|Eiryaku]] 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|qq=}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|qq=}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō Ward]], Kyoto City. Its former Rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|qq=}}. It was once the guardian shrine of [[:en:Zenrin-ji|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji]] and is situated at the southern end of [[:en:Philosopher's Walk|Philosopher's Walk]]. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|qq=}}.
== History ==
In [[:en:Eiryaku|Eiryaku]] 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} at [[:en:Zenrin-ji|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji]], establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Ōji|qq=}}, an alternative name for [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]].
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pilgrimages to Kumano|qq=}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugenja|qq=}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
[[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and later, in [[:en:Kanshō|Kanshō]] 6 (1465), [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimasa|Ashikaga Yoshimasa]], held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], it was later restored by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]].
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|qq=}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—[[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]], [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], and [[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]]—but in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred [[:en:Nageia nagi|Nagi tree]], estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-Kumano Shrine|qq=}}, associated with [[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]]; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}}, associated with [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]].
== Deities ==
* [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]] * [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] * [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] * [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Sources ==
* Shrine history from Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine.

* [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]] * [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] * [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] * [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]], [[:en:Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō Ward]], 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= [[File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg|260px|Haiden]] | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]], [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]], [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] |Established = [[:en:Eiryaku|Eiryaku]] 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|qq=}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|qq=}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō Ward]], Kyoto City. Its former Rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|qq=}}. It was once the guardian shrine of [[:en:Zenrin-ji|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji]] and is situated at the southern end of [[:en:Philosopher's Walk|Philosopher's Walk]]. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|qq=}}.
== History ==
In [[:en:Eiryaku|Eiryaku]] 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qq=}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} at [[:en:Zenrin-ji|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji]], establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Ōji|qq=}}, an alternative name for [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]].
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pilgrimages to Kumano|qq=}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugenja|qq=}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
[[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and later, in [[:en:Kanshō|Kanshō]] 6 (1465), [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimasa|Ashikaga Yoshimasa]], held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}} and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], it was later restored by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]].
During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|qq=}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—[[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]], [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], and [[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]]—but in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred [[:en:Nageia nagi|Nagi tree]], estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-Kumano Shrine|qq=}}, associated with [[:en:Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongū Taisha]]; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}}, associated with [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]].
== Deities ==
* [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]] * [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] * [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] * [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office

* Annual Festival – July 10
