Its former Rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Zenrin-ji|en|Zenrin-ji|WD=Q189807|lt=Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|1=Zenrin-ji}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|en|Philosopher's Walk|WD=Q1510488|lt=Philosopher's Walk|1=Philosopher's Walk}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135506332|1=Q135506332}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|en|Eiryaku|WD=Q1193895|lt=Eiryaku|1=Eiryaku}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} at {{ill|Zenrin-ji|en|Zenrin-ji|WD=Q189807|lt=Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|1=Zenrin-ji}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Ōji|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pilgrimages to Kumano|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Three Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugenja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏|1=Yamabushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2309838}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|en|Kanshō|WD=Q1143928|lt=Kanshō|1=Kanshō}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|WD=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa|1=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the [[Ashikaga Shogunate]] and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|WD=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|WD=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya|1=Wakamiya}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nageia nagi|en|Nageia nagi|WD=Q3274110|lt=Nagi tree|1=Nageia nagi}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-Kumano Shrine|1=Imakumano Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17193550}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|WD=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Kumano Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q106852642}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}.

Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135506332|1=Q135506332}}.

In {{ill|Eiryaku|en|Eiryaku|WD=Q1193895|lt=Eiryaku|1=Eiryaku}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|WD=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} at {{ill|Zenrin-ji|en|Zenrin-ji|WD=Q189807|lt=Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|1=Zenrin-ji}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Ōji|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}.

{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|en|Kanshō|WD=Q1143928|lt=Kanshō|1=Kanshō}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|WD=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa|1=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the [[Ashikaga Shogunate]] and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|WD=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|WD=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya|1=Wakamiya}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nageia nagi|en|Nageia nagi|WD=Q3274110|lt=Nagi tree|1=Nageia nagi}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-Kumano Shrine|1=Imakumano Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17193550}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|WD=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Kumano Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q106852642}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}.

During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|WD=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|WD=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya|1=Wakamiya}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nageia nagi|en|Nageia nagi|WD=Q3274110|lt=Nagi tree|1=Nageia nagi}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-Kumano Shrine|1=Imakumano Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17193550}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|WD=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Kumano Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q106852642}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|WD=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}.
== Deities ==

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō Ward|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|en|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|qid=Q478504|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}, {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanagi|1=Izanagi}}, {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|en|Eiryaku|qid=Q1193895|lt=Eiryaku|1=Eiryaku}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = |location = {{ill|Sakyō-ku|qid=Q1321832|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35.015691|N|135.796308|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|qq=}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō Ward|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, Kyoto City. Its former Rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Zenrin-ji|en|Zenrin-ji|qid=Q189807|lt=Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|1=Zenrin-ji}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|en|Philosopher's Walk|qid=Q1510488|lt=Philosopher's Walk|1=Philosopher's Walk}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135506332|1=Q135506332}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|en|Eiryaku|qid=Q1193895|lt=Eiryaku|1=Eiryaku}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|qid=Q317984|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|qid=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} at {{ill|Zenrin-ji|en|Zenrin-ji|qid=Q189807|lt=Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|1=Zenrin-ji}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Ōji|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11616186}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pilgrimages to Kumano|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Three Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10403700}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugenja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏|1=Yamabushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2309838}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|qid=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|en|Kanshō|qid=Q1143928|lt=Kanshō|1=Kanshō}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|qid=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa|1=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the [[Ashikaga Shogunate]] and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|qid=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qid=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|qid=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|qid=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya|1=Wakamiya}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nageia nagi|en|Nageia nagi|qid=Q3274110|lt=Nagi tree|1=Nageia nagi}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-Kumano Shrine|1=Imakumano Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17193550}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|qid=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Kumano Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q106852642}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qid=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|qid=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}.
== Deities ==

== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office

== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|en|Eiryaku|qid=Q1193895|lt=Eiryaku|1=Eiryaku}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|qid=Q317984|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|qid=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} at {{ill|Zenrin-ji|en|Zenrin-ji|qid=Q189807|lt=Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|1=Zenrin-ji}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Ōji|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11616186}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pilgrimages to Kumano|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Three Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10403700}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugenja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏|1=Yamabushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2309838}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|qid=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|en|Kanshō|qid=Q1143928|lt=Kanshō|1=Kanshō}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|qid=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa|1=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the [[Ashikaga Shogunate]] and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|qid=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qid=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|qid=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|qid=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya|1=Wakamiya}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nageia nagi|en|Nageia nagi|qid=Q3274110|lt=Nagi tree|1=Nageia nagi}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-Kumano Shrine|1=Imakumano Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17193550}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|qid=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Kumano Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q106852642}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qid=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|qid=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name= Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine |location= {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō Ward|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, 2 Wakaoji-chō |image= File:Kumano-nyakuoji-jinja.jpg | Latitude = | Longitude = | Country Code = |deity = {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|en|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|qid=Q478504|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}, {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanagi|1=Izanagi}}, {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} |Established = {{ill|Eiryaku|en|Eiryaku|qid=Q1193895|lt=Eiryaku|1=Eiryaku}} 1 (1160) |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} |Pilgrimage = |Main Hall = |Festival = July 10 |Rituals = |Map = |location = {{ill|Sakyō-ku|qid=Q1321832|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35.015691|N|135.796308|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''{{ill|Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|10=ja|11=熊野若王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine|qq=}}''' (熊野若王子神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō Ward|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, Kyoto City. Its former Rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. It was once the guardian shrine of {{ill|Zenrin-ji|en|Zenrin-ji|qid=Q189807|lt=Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|1=Zenrin-ji}} and is situated at the southern end of {{ill|Philosopher's Walk|en|Philosopher's Walk|qid=Q1510488|lt=Philosopher's Walk|1=Philosopher's Walk}}. Commonly referred to as Nyakuōji Shrine, it is one of the {{ill|Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja|京都三熊野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto's Three Kumano Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135506332|1=Q135506332}}.
== History ==
In {{ill|Eiryaku|en|Eiryaku|qid=Q1193895|lt=Eiryaku|1=Eiryaku}} 1 (1160), {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|qid=Q317984|ja|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|2=ja|3=後白河天皇|qid=Q317984|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Shirakawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} at {{ill|Zenrin-ji|en|Zenrin-ji|qid=Q189807|lt=Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|1=Zenrin-ji}}, establishing the shrine. The shrine’s name is derived from {{ill|Wakamiya Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamiya Ōji|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11616186}}, an alternative name for {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}.
In preparation for {{ill|pilgrimages to Kumano|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pilgrimages to Kumano|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Three Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10403700}}, {{ill|Shugenja|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugenja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏|1=Yamabushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2309838}} were said to purify themselves at this shrine before embarking on their journey.
{{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|qid=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and later, in {{ill|Kanshō|en|Kanshō|qid=Q1143928|lt=Kanshō|1=Kanshō}} 6 (1465), {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|qid=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa|1=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}}, held a cherry blossom viewing party at the shrine. The shrine enjoyed patronage from the [[Ashikaga Shogunate]] and various samurai clans.
Though the shrine fell into ruin during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, it was later restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}.
During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine buildings had deteriorated and were repaired. It was designated a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. Previously, the main shrine consisted of four sub-shrines—{{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|qid=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}, {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qid=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}, {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|qid=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, and {{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|qid=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya|1=Wakamiya}}—but in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 54 (1979), they were consolidated into a single shrine.
The shrine is home to Kyoto Prefecture’s oldest sacred {{ill|Nageia nagi|en|Nageia nagi|qid=Q3274110|lt=Nagi tree|1=Nageia nagi}}, estimated to be over 400 years old.
Kyoto is home to the three Kumano Shrines: {{ill|Shin-Kumano Shrine|ja|新熊野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-Kumano Shrine|1=Imakumano Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17193550}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|qid=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}; {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|熊野神社 (京都市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Kumano Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q106852642}}, associated with {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qid=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}; and Kumano Nyakuōji Shrine, associated with {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|qid=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}.
== Deities ==
* {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|en|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|qid=Q478504|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}} * {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanagi|1=Izanagi}} * {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} * {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}
== Grounds ==
* Main Hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 54 (1979). * Worship Hall * Ebisu Shrine * Shrine Office
== Festivals ==
* Annual Festival – July 10
